purpose: Tag-triggered release pipeline. On any tag matching v[0-9]*: builds release on Windows + Linux in parallel, zips/tars the binaries (with the tag name in the filename), extracts the matching CHANGELOG section, and publishes a GitHub Release with both artefacts attached.
invariants: Tag pattern is v[0-9]* — any tag starting with v followed by a digit triggers a release. Renaming to a non-matching pattern (e.g. just '1.0.0') silently no-ops.
Windows artifact path: build/release/LightHost_artefacts/Release/Light Host.exe — set by JUCE's juce_add_gui_app + the VS2026 multi-config generator. Note the literal space in 'Light Host.exe'.

Linux artifact path: build/ninja-release/LightHost_artefacts/Release/Light Host — JUCE places the binary under <artefacts>/Release/ even for single-config Ninja (this was a fix in commit 95c84d8 after the original path was wrong).
Release notes are extracted from CHANGELOG.md via awk slicing between '## [<version>]' headers — the tag is v-prefixed but the CHANGELOG entry is not, so VER strips the leading v before grepping.
create-release job needs `permissions: contents: write` to create a GitHub Release; without it softprops/action-gh-release@v2 fails with a 403.
gotchas: Windows ZIP contains only `Light Host.exe` — no licence, no README, no resources. The exe is self-contained but users won't see docs inside the archive.
Linux tarball wraps the binary without surrounding files for the same reason; the binary has a literal space in its name which can confuse some shell pipelines on the consumer side.
If CHANGELOG.md has no matching '## [<version>]' header for the tag, release_notes.md is empty and the GitHub Release is published with an empty body. Verify the CHANGELOG before tagging.

Artifact retention is 1 day — the GitHub Release itself retains them permanently, but the intermediate upload-artifact entries used for the cross-job handoff disappear quickly. Re-running the workflow without re-tagging requires re-uploads.
